critic_raw:
Fair Selective Classification via Sufficiency introduces a mutual information regularizer to enforce sufficiency — the condition that the predicted label is independent of the sensitive attribute given the true label. Mutual information is notoriously difficult to estimate in high dimensions from finite samples; the plug-in estimators (histogram-based, kernel-based, or neural-network-based) are all biased, and the bias is data-dependent. If the mutual information regularizer is estimated with a biased estimator, the model is being trained to minimize a biased proxy of the sufficiency criterion, which may produce a model that appears to satisfy sufficiency on training data while violating it in regions where the estimator is poorly calibrated.

Sufficiency is one of several established fairness criteria (alongside demographic parity, equalized odds, individual fairness), and these criteria are provably in tension — satisfying one can require violating others. The paper's claim that the method improves fairness without reporting its effect on other standard fairness criteria leaves open the possibility that the method trades one fairness violation for another. In particular, sufficiency-regularized models that equalize precision across groups may do so by sacrificing recall disparities, which the description does not address.

The claim that 'the sufficiency approach may decrease overall accuracy in certain instances but continues to reduce disparities' needs to be evaluated at matched accuracy levels between the sufficiency-regularized model and the baseline. If accuracy is allowed to differ, improvements in fairness metrics may simply reflect a less discriminative model — any model that abstains on uncertain cases will reduce disparities, but this is not a fairness achievement, it is a precision-coverage tradeoff. The claim requires controlling for coverage and accuracy simultaneously.
defender_raw:
Fair selective classification addresses a problem that standard fairness methods largely ignore: what happens to fairness when the classifier is allowed to abstain on uncertain cases? Standard fairness metrics (equal opportunity, demographic parity) are defined over all predictions, but in selective classification, the classifier can refuse to predict for cases where its confidence is low. This abstention can introduce or amplify disparities if the classifier abstains more frequently for one demographic group than another.

The sufficiency criterion as a fairness constraint is well-motivated. Sufficiency requires that the prediction be conditionally independent of the protected attribute given the classifier's score — i.e., among all samples with the same predicted probability, the true label distribution should be the same across groups. This is a natural fairness criterion for selective classification because it ensures that the precision of the classifier is equal across groups at every coverage level, not just in aggregate.

The mutual information regularizer for enforcing sufficiency is a principled choice. Mutual information directly measures statistical dependence between the prediction and the protected attribute given the score, which is exactly what sufficiency requires. Alternative regularizers (e.g., L1/L2 penalties on group-specific precision differences) are less principled because they require defining a specific functional form for the dependence.

The claim that the method reduces precision disparities across the entire coverage scale — not just at a specific operating point — is an important strengthening of the standard fairness claim. Many fairness methods demonstrate equity at a single operating point but do not maintain it as the coverage threshold varies. Evaluating across the full coverage scale is the correct evaluation protocol for selective classification.

Evaluating on four datasets with different data types provides breadth of evidence. Fairness methods are often sensitive to dataset-specific characteristics (label imbalance, group size disparities), and multi-dataset evaluation establishes that the sufficiency regularizer provides consistent fairness improvements rather than exploiting specific dataset properties.
all_issues_raised: Mutual information estimation in high dimensions is biased with all practical estimators; training with a biased MI regularizer optimizes a proxy of sufficiency that may not correspond to the actual criterion, particularly in low-data regions where the estimator is least accurate.

Sufficiency is in provable tension with other fairness criteria (demographic parity, equalized odds); reporting only sufficiency-related improvements without assessing other fairness criteria leaves open the possibility of trading one fairness violation for another.

Fairness improvement comparisons must control for coverage and overall accuracy simultaneously; a less discriminative model will mechanically reduce disparity by abstaining more broadly, and this conflation is not addressed.
all_issues_adjudicated: Mutual information estimation in high dimensions is biased with all practical estimators; training with a biased MI regularizer optimizes a proxy of sufficiency that may not correspond to the actual criterion, particularly in low-data regions where the estimator is least accurate.

Sufficiency is in provable tension with other fairness criteria (demographic parity, equalized odds); reporting only sufficiency-related improvements without assessing other fairness criteria leaves open the possibility of trading one fairness violation for another.

Fairness improvement comparisons must control for coverage and overall accuracy simultaneously; a less discriminative model will mechanically reduce disparity by abstaining more broadly, and this conflation is not addressed.
